# Representing Individual Constituents

```{r knit-options, include=FALSE, cache=FALSE}
type = 1
load(here::here("data", type, "means.Rdata"))
here::here("code", "formatting.R") |> source()
```


> ABSTRACT: We examine what explains the unequal levels of constituency service that legislators provide to individuals in their district. Folk theory explanations about district demand and, especially, legislator capacity explain a large share of the variation. We find that constituency service work is most prominently driven by legislators’ experience in office. However, constituency service is not free of partisan and ideological influences. Legislators also do more constituency service with agencies with which they are ideologically aligned or that fall under the oversight jurisdiction of their committee assignment. Interestingly, and unlike other types of contacts with federal agencies, constituency service is highly correlated with legislative effectiveness, not just at the top end of the distribution but also at the bottom. There is no trade-off between legislative effectiveness and constituency service (as suggested by theories supporting term limits); indeed, we find they are highly correlated.

<!-- Ellie: Potential Block quote to include -->

> An individual complaint or request for a favor may itself be trivial, except to the aggreived or hopeful citizen and his family. Responding to it may tax the energy and ingenuity of an over-worked member of Congress and his staff. No single request left unheeded is likely to rock the Republic. But the provision of millions of such miniscule services, in their totality, amount to a major function of modern American government. How well these services are performed goes a long way in determining the confidence of citizens in their representatives.
>
> --- @Olson1966, pg. 338.


Legislators do more constituency service with agencies with whom they are ideologically aligned or sit on the oversight committee.


Unlike other types of contacts with federal agencies, constituency service is highly correlated with legislative effectiveness, not just at the top end of the distribution but also at the bottom. Legislators who contact agencies most often tend to have high LES scores. Those who contact agencies the least tend to have low LES scores.


## What it looks like


```{r}
#| label: fig-words
#| fig-cap: "Most frequent words by year"

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", "wordcloud", type, "overall_by_year.png"))
```

We conceptualize constituency service behalf of individuals as including groups of individuals; in few cases, large numbers of people. For example, Marco Rubio wrote about people across Florida receiving FEMA disaster aid. 

Given the large number of topics in which the federal bureaucracy is involved, constituency service on behalf of individuals involves legislators' contacting a number of different agencies for a vast array of purposes. Many of the most common will be familiar to scholars of congressional representation and constituency service. As evidenced by the word cloud in @fig-words, our data include a significant number of contacts between legislators and the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to ask about the status of claims that their veteran constituents had filed with the VA. Other common subjects of requests to the VA on behalf of individual constituents involved appeals, disability ratings, and changes in benefits. Interestingly, a fair number of VA letters were identified as follow-up requests, indicating that legislators' casework staff were repeatedly contacting the VA about the same constituent case. Determining whether this pattern speaks more to a particular unresponsiveness of the VA or exceptional persistence on the part of congressional staff (or exceptionally detailed record-keeping by the VA) is beyond the scope of our analysis, but it does accord with our theoretical expectation about the importance of capacity for interbranch representation.

Similar communications appeared in the congressional correspondence logs from many sub-agencies in the Department of Labor (DOL). Several different DOL sub-agencies, including the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), the Office of Workers' Compensation Programs (OWCP), and the Office of Congressional and Intergovernmental Affairs (OCIA), handled congressional requests involving on-the-job injuries, benefits claims, and workplace safety complaints. Also unsurprising is the number of communications with the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) in the Treasury Department on behalf of constituents dealing with issues involving their taxes, especially tax refunds. Though not evident in the word cloud because they were identified in the logs using the names of specific forms, a very large amount of individual constituency service requests involved immigration issues and were sent to U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) within the Department of Homeland Security.

Beyond these "classic" examples of individual constituency service, there are also many examples of correspondence on behalf of individuals that did not involve a problem with the federal bureaucracy (at least not in the sense that the bureaucracy had made an error or the constituent had experienced some injustice). The National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) received thousands of requests from legislators on behalf of constituents who were seeking copies of their family members' military records. Many legislators also forwarded constituent complaints and queries to the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) within the Department of Health and Human Services. Although these concerns were broadly related to the policy work of the FDA, they often did not suggest a specific bureaucratic failure on the part of the agency. For example, Senator Arlen Specter wrote to the FDA with a request to investigate the treatment that a constituent received at a VA hospital. The constituent had an adverse reaction to a drug that included joint pain, memory loss, sleep apnea, vision loss, and stomach difficulties. The symptoms abated after the patient stopped taking the drug, at the behest of the VA physician. At issue in this case was the drug, not the VA, as the physician seemed to recognize and respond to the problem. And although the FDA had the ability to investigate the issue, the constituent was not contacting Specter about a particular bureaucratic issue with the FDA. This example illustrates how problems can sometimes appear for constituents at the intersection of several agencies, which suggests that the expertise and capacity of legislators' offices matters for addressing these issues. Congressional staff need to have some sense of how contacting an agency may help to address a problem in a way that is appropriate for the constituent, and these ambiguous cases likely require deep knowledge of the federal bureaucracy. 


```{r}
#| label: fig-percent
#| fig-cap: "Observed levels of interbranch representation per legislator per year"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - "Senate"
#|   - "House"
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "percentile-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "percentile-2.png"))

```


## Explaining Differences in Constituency Service  

Variation in constituency service is mainly driven by ... 

### Demand

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-demand
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of demand on requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 100%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_demand.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_demand,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates the relationship between district demographics that may affect demand and levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```




```{r}
#| label: fig-pop-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per Senator per year compared to counterfactual of equal state population"
#| out-width: 50%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-population-Senate-effect-1.png"))

#TOD college grads and government workers 

```


### Capacity

Columns 1, 3, and 5 of @tbl-models-demand) show differences across legislators with different capacity advantages. 

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-capacity
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of capacity on total requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 100%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_capacity.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_capacity,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of legislator capacity (committee chair, subcommittee chair, and ranking member positions and years in office) and institutional positions that may affect strategic behavior (oversight committee membership, majority party status, co-partisan president) on levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per legislator per year compared to counterfactuals"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(
here::here("figs", type,
           paste0("m-effect-",
                  c(1:2,5,8), 
                  ".png"
                  ) 
           ))
```




##### Staff quality 

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-district
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of capacity on total requests to federal agencies per district"

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_district.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_district,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by district",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of electing a new member of the same or different party on levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```


### Strategic Responses to the Environment


#### Interbranch representation as electoral strategy


```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-elect
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of electoral security on total requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 60%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_elect.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_elect,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of electoral competition on levels of interbranch representation. "))  

```


```{r}
#| label: fig-elect-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactual of no electoral competition"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-elect-effect-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-elect-effect-2.png"))

```



#### Strategic responses to partisan institutions


 


```{r}
#| label: fig-ideo-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactual of no difference in legislator ideology (a) and perceived agency ideology (b)"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-ideo-effect-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-ideo-effect-2.png"))
```




```{r}
#| label: fig-partisan-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactuals of no partisan effects"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-effect-6.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-effect-7.png"))
```




#### Staff allocation reflects priories


```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-staff
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of staff budgeting on requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 100%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_staff_spending.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_staff_spending,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of legislator staff spending. ")) 
```



```{r}
#| label: fig-effect-staff
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per representative per year compared to counterfactual staffing levels"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(
here::here("figs", type,
           paste0("m-staff_spending-effect-",
                  c(1:2), 
                  ".png"
                  ) 
           ))
```



#### Interbranch representation and campaign fundraising strategy

[possibly only have this section in chapters 7 and 9]

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-pac
#| tbl-cap: "Legislator requests to federal agencies and Corporate Political Action Committee Contributions in the Prior Congress" 

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_pac.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_pac,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by district",
                          "This table shows estimates of the relationship between corporate Political Action Committee donations and levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```
